heading: Assessment
html:
Identify frailty in adults with multimorbidity, falls risk, frequent unplanned care use, or high treatment burden. Frailty assessment should be practical, repeatable, and linked to action.

  • Consider PRISMA-7, gait speed assessment, or self-rated health status alongside clinical judgement.
  • Assess function, mobility, ADLs, cognition, mood, nutrition, hydration, continence, and social support.
  • Review recent stressors (infection, medication changes, falls, hospital discharge) and trajectory of decline or recovery.
  • Assess treatment burden, including medicine count and complexity, appointment load, and adherence barriers.

heading: Primary care management
html:
The default approach is proactive, person-centred, and coordinated care aimed at preserving independence and reducing avoidable harm.

  • Individualised management plan: agree goals, priorities, key risks, and named care coordinator.
  • Medication optimisation: reconcile all medicines, reduce low-value or high-risk prescribing, simplify regimens, and monitor after changes.
  • Falls prevention: assess falls history, postural blood pressure, medicine contributors, mobility and home risk; link with local falls services.
  • Nutrition and hydration: identify weight loss/poor intake early and involve community dietetic or therapy support where needed.
  • Cognition and mental health: screen for delirium risk, cognitive decline, and depression/anxiety; address reversible contributors.
  • Review schedule: increase frequency after deterioration or medication changes; provide clear safety-net and urgent-contact advice.

heading: Who to refer
html:
Urgent same-day referral

  • Acute delirium, suspected sepsis, major injury/fracture, or rapid functional collapse.
  • Unsafe home situation due to immediate carer breakdown or safeguarding concern.
  • Acute inability to maintain hydration/nutrition or inability to cope safely at home.

Routine referral

  • Progressive frailty with recurrent falls or repeated unplanned hospital use.
  • Complex polypharmacy or treatment-burden decisions beyond routine primary care optimisation.
  • Need for comprehensive geriatric assessment or coordinated MDT planning.

Include in referral

  • Frailty assessment method and score/clinical impression.
  • Functional baseline and recent trajectory.
  • Falls history, medicines list and recent changes, and social/carer context.

heading: Who not to refer
html:
Do not routinely refer people with stable mild frailty whose needs are being met in primary care and community services, with no red flags and no rapid decline.

Continue structured proactive review, medication optimisation, falls prevention, and timely escalation if risk increases.

heading: References
html:
• NICE NG56 recommendations: multimorbidity and frailty assessment/care
  • NICE NG249: falls assessment and prevention
  • British Geriatrics Society: Introduction to Frailty
